rationale: Our favorable military balance and limited territorial gains justify targeted offensive pressure to degrade enemy defenses before they consolidate.
The near-collision incident reveals adversary overreach; a visible naval presence deters escalation and protects maritime interests.
Reinforcing the northern front preempts counteroffensives and exploits current momentum while Tethys remains disorganized.
With the recent cyber incident on our grid, retaliatory cyber operations can exploit Tethys’s likely weakened cyber posture and disrupt command systems.
Adversary logistics in occupied zones are vulnerable; sabotage operations can erode their operational endurance without direct confrontation.
Amplifying internal doubts about Tethys’s defense capabilities undermines civilian morale and strains their war economy.
With sanctions at 31% and gas prices surging, securing critical energy and supply reserves is essential to sustain prolonged conflict.
Leverage 69% international support to tighten coalition-aligned restrictions on dual-use exports to Novaris, amplifying economic pressure.
Domestic financing through war bonds insulates the budget from sanction spillover and inflationary pressures caused by the energy crisis.
With Novaris holding territorial gains and military balance unfavorable, reinforcing defensive positions is critical to halt further advances.
The naval incident involving a neutral tanker necessitates increased maritime vigilance to deter escalation and protect sea lanes.
Conducting visible drills near contested zones signals resolve to both adversary and allies without provoking direct escalation.
In response to the suspected cyber attack on our power grid, immediate hardening of critical infrastructure networks is essential to prevent cascading failures.
Monitoring enemy command channels will provide early warning of further cyber or physical escalation, especially given intelligence of impending operations.
Seed false intelligence about troop movements to divert enemy resources and buy time for defensive consolidation.
With 69% international support, now is the time to formalize alliances and coordinate responses to the peace initiative and naval incident.
While publicly supporting mediation, discreet talks can test Novaris’s true intentions and exploit potential fractures in their campaign.
Deploying neutral monitors would bolster legitimacy and deter further unilateral actions by Novaris under the guise of the peace framework.
With gas prices surging 25% and sanctions at 31%, securing energy and critical supplies now will buffer against future shocks and sustain war readiness.
Leverage high international support to lock in emergency energy and logistics deals with key allies ahead of potential supply disruptions.
Coordinate with coalition partners to restrict dual-use technology exports, amplifying economic pressure without triggering uncontrolled escalation.
